title: Panna cotta
subtitle: Set Italian cream with vanilla and a berry sauce.
excerpt: Cream and milk sweetened with sugar and perfumed with vanilla, set with gelatine into a trembling, barely-solid pudding that turns out onto a plate and holds just enough shape to slice. Served with sharp berry coulis. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Panna cotta. The name means 'cooked cream' in Italian and the dessert originates in the Piedmont region of northern Italy, where it has been made since at least the early 20th century. It became internationally popular from the 1990s onwards as Italian regional cooking gained a wider audience. Recipes appear in Marcella Hazan's works and in Giorgio Locatelli's Made in Italy (2006).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before; the texture improves overnight. Unmould just before serving.

content.text: Panna cotta should wobble. A firm, sliceable panna cotta is over-set; it should barely hold its shape on the plate. The gelatine quantity here produces the correct texture: 2.5 leaves for 600 ml of dairy. Use individual small cups, glasses or moulds oiled lightly with a neutral oil so they turn out cleanly.

items.prepNote: split and scraped; or 1 tsp vanilla extract
soaked in cold water for 5 minutes

content.text: Warm {{double-cream}} of double cream, {{whole-milk}} of whole milk, {{caster-sugar}} of sugar and {{vanilla-pod}} vanilla pod in a saucepan over gentle heat until steaming but not boiling. Squeeze the soaked {{gelatine-leaves}} gelatine leaves dry and stir into the hot cream until fully dissolved. Remove the vanilla pod, pour into lightly oiled moulds and refrigerate for at least 4 hours.

For the coulis, warm {{mixed-frozen-berries}} of frozen berries with {{caster-sugar}} of sugar and the juice of {{lemon}} lemon until the berries collapse. Sieve or blend. To serve, run a knife around the edge of each mould and turn out onto a plate. Spoon the coulis around.

items.cause: Too much gelatine or left to set too long.
items.fix: Three leaves is the right quantity for this recipe. The correct set is a pronounced wobble; a firm block means an extra half-leaf of gelatine.

items.symptom: Gelatine lumps visible in the finished pudding
items.cause: Cream too cold when gelatine added, or gelatine not fully squeezed before adding.
items.fix: The cream must be hot (steaming) when the gelatine goes in. Squeeze out every drop of water first.

items.symptom: Panna cotta will not turn out cleanly
items.cause: Moulds not oiled, or not chilled long enough.
items.fix: Oil lightly with a neutral oil before filling. A quick dip in warm water for 3 seconds helps release a reluctant panna cotta.

content.text: Panna cotta is the most elegant of the easy desserts: nothing is cooked in any real sense, nothing is difficult, and the result looks and tastes restaurant-quality. It travels from the Piedmont kitchen to any dining table. The coulis is optional but makes the presentation. A squeeze of passion fruit or a few sliced strawberries serve equally well.
